sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「六藝於治一也。

translations.literal: Confucius said: "The Six Arts are united in governance."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said: "The Six Arts all serve a single purpose in governance."

sentences.zh: 禮以節人，樂以發和，書以道事，詩以達意，易以神化，春秋以義。」

translations.literal: "Ritual regulates people, Music brings forth harmony, the Documents speak of affairs, the Odes convey meaning, the Changes work spiritual transformation, and the Spring and Autumn Annals uphold righteousness."
translations.idiomatic: "The Rites temper human conduct, Music inspires harmony, the Documents illuminate affairs of state, the Odes give voice to the heart's intent, the Changes reveal the workings of the divine, and the Spring and Autumn Annals uphold righteousness."

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：天道恢恢，豈不大哉！

translations.literal: The Grand Historian says: The Way of Heaven is vast and all-encompassing — is it not great!
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: How vast and all-embracing is the Way of Heaven!

sentences.zh: 談言微中，亦可以解紛。

translations.literal: Casual words that subtly hit the mark can also resolve disputes.
translations.idiomatic: A well-placed jest, hitting subtly upon the truth, can untangle even the most knotted affairs.

sentences.zh: 淳于髡者，齊之贅婿也。

translations.literal: Chunyu Kun was a live-in son-in-law from the state of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Kun was a man of Qi who had married into his wife's family as a live-in son-in-law.

sentences.zh: 長不滿七尺，滑稽多辯，數使諸侯，未嘗屈辱。

translations.literal: He stood less than seven chi tall, was witty and eloquent, and served as envoy to the feudal lords many times without ever being humiliated.
translations.idiomatic: He stood less than seven chi tall, yet he was quick-witted and sharp-tongued. Sent on missions to the feudal lords time and again, he never once suffered disgrace.

sentences.zh: 齊威王之時喜隱，好為淫樂長夜之飲，沈湎不治，委政卿大夫。

translations.literal: During the time of King Wei of Qi, the king was fond of riddles, indulged in excessive pleasures and all-night drinking, wallowed in dissipation and neglected governance, delegating affairs of state to his ministers and grandees.
translations.idiomatic: King Wei of Qi delighted in riddles. He gave himself over to pleasure and caroused through the night, sinking into dissipation and neglecting the affairs of state, which he left entirely to his ministers.

sentences.zh: 百官荒亂，諸侯并侵，國且危亡，在於旦暮，左右莫敢諫。

translations.literal: The hundred officials fell into disorder, the feudal lords encroached from all sides, the state was on the verge of collapse — its destruction could come any morning or evening — yet none around the king dared to remonstrate.
translations.idiomatic: The court officials descended into chaos, rival lords invaded from every quarter, and the state teetered on the brink of ruin — its fall a matter of days. Yet not one of the king's attendants dared speak a word of remonstrance.

sentences.zh: 淳于髡說之以隱曰：「國中有大鳥，止王之庭，三年不蜚又不鳴，不知此鳥何也？」

translations.literal: Chunyu Kun persuaded the king with a riddle, saying: "There is a great bird in the kingdom that has perched in the king's court for three years without flying or crying out — do you know what bird this is?"
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Kun approached the king with a riddle: "In our kingdom there is a great bird that has roosted in Your Majesty's courtyard for three full years. It neither flies nor sings. What manner of bird might this be?"

sentences.zh: 王曰：「此鳥不飛則已，一飛沖天；

translations.literal: The king said: "This bird — if it does not fly, then so be it, but once it flies it will soar to the heavens;"
translations.idiomatic: The king replied: "This bird — if it does not fly, so be it. But once it takes flight, it will soar straight to the heavens."

sentences.zh: 不鳴則已，一鳴驚人。」

translations.literal: "If it does not cry then so be it, but once it cries it will astound everyone."
translations.idiomatic: "If it does not sing, so be it. But once it cries out, it will astonish the world."

sentences.zh: 於是乃朝諸縣令長七十二人，賞一人，誅一人，奮兵而出。

translations.literal: Thereupon he summoned the seventy-two county magistrates to court, rewarded one, executed one, and mobilized his armies to march forth.
translations.idiomatic: With that, the king summoned his seventy-two county magistrates to court. He rewarded one and put another to death, then roused his armies and marched forth.

sentences.zh: 諸侯振驚，皆還齊侵地。

translations.literal: The feudal lords were shaken and alarmed, and all returned the territories they had seized from Qi.
translations.idiomatic: The feudal lords were shaken with alarm. Every one of them returned the lands they had seized from Qi.

sentences.zh: 威行三十六年。

translations.literal: His prestige lasted for thirty-six years.
translations.idiomatic: His authority held sway for thirty-six years.

sentences.zh: 語在田完世家中。

translations.literal: This account is in the Hereditary House of Tian Wan.
translations.idiomatic: The full account can be found in the Hereditary House of Lord Tian Wan.

sentences.zh: 威王八年，楚大發兵加齊。

translations.literal: In the eighth year of King Wei, Chu launched a great army against Qi.
translations.idiomatic: In the eighth year of King Wei's reign, Chu mobilized a massive army and attacked Qi.

sentences.zh: 齊王使淳于髡之趙請救兵，齎金百斤，車馬十駟。

translations.literal: The King of Qi sent Chunyu Kun to Zhao to request rescue troops, bearing a hundred catties of gold and ten four-horse chariots.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Qi dispatched Chunyu Kun to Zhao to beg for reinforcements, equipping him with a hundred catties of gold and ten teams of horses with chariots.

sentences.zh: 淳于髡仰天大笑，冠纓索絕。

translations.literal: Chunyu Kun threw back his head toward the sky and laughed greatly, until the strings of his cap snapped.
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Kun threw back his head and roared with laughter — so hard that the chin-straps of his cap snapped clean through.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「先生少之乎？」

translations.literal: The king said: "Does the master consider it too little?"
translations.idiomatic: The king asked: "Does the master find the gifts insufficient?"

sentences.zh: 髡曰：「何敢！」

translations.literal: Kun said: "How would I dare!"
translations.idiomatic: Kun replied: "I would never dare!"

sentences.zh: 王曰：「笑豈有說乎？」

translations.literal: The king said: "Does the laugh have an explanation?"
translations.idiomatic: The king pressed: "Then what was the meaning of that laugh?"

sentences.zh: 髡曰：「今者臣從東方來，見道傍有禳田者，操一豚蹄，酒一盂，祝曰：『甌窶滿篝，汙邪滿車，五穀蕃熟，穰穰滿家。』

translations.literal: Kun said: "Just now your servant was coming from the east, and saw by the roadside a man performing a ritual to protect his fields, holding a single pig's trotter and a bowl of wine, praying: 'May the high-ground baskets overflow, may the lowland carts be heaped full, may the five grains ripen in abundance, may bountiful harvests fill my house.'"
translations.idiomatic: Kun said: "Just now, as your servant was traveling from the east, I saw a man by the roadside performing a prayer over his fields. He held up a single pig's trotter and a bowl of wine and prayed: 'Let the hilltop baskets overflow! Let the lowland carts be piled high! Let the five grains ripen in plenty, and harvests fill my house!'"

sentences.zh: 臣見其所持者狹而所欲者奢，故笑之。」

translations.literal: "Your servant saw that what he offered was meager while what he desired was extravagant, and so I laughed at him."
translations.idiomatic: "I saw that his offering was paltry but his prayers were grand — and so I could not help but laugh."

sentences.zh: 於是齊威王乃益齎黃金千溢，白璧十雙，車馬百駟。

translations.literal: Thereupon King Wei of Qi increased the gifts to a thousand yi of gold, ten pairs of white jade discs, and a hundred four-horse chariots.
translations.idiomatic: At this, King Wei increased the gifts tenfold: a thousand yi of gold, ten pairs of white jade discs, and a hundred teams of horses with chariots.

sentences.zh: 髡辭而行，至趙。

translations.literal: Kun took his leave and traveled, arriving in Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Kun took his leave and set out for Zhao.

sentences.zh: 趙王與之精兵十萬，革車千乘。

translations.literal: The King of Zhao gave him one hundred thousand elite soldiers and a thousand leather-covered war chariots.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Zhao provided a hundred thousand crack troops and a thousand war chariots.

sentences.zh: 楚聞之，夜引兵而去。

translations.literal: When Chu heard of this, they withdrew their army at night and left.
translations.idiomatic: When Chu received word, they pulled back their forces under cover of night.

sentences.zh: 威王大說，置酒後宮，召髡賜之酒。

translations.literal: King Wei was greatly pleased, held a banquet in the rear palace, summoned Kun and bestowed wine upon him.
translations.idiomatic: King Wei was overjoyed. He laid out a feast in the inner palace and summoned Kun to share in the wine.

sentences.zh: 問曰：「先生能飲幾何而醉？」

translations.literal: He asked: "How much can the master drink before becoming drunk?"
translations.idiomatic: He asked: "How much can the master drink before he gets drunk?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣飲一斗亦醉，一石亦醉。」

translations.literal: He replied: "Your servant becomes drunk on one dou, and also becomes drunk on one dan."
translations.idiomatic: Kun answered: "Your servant gets drunk after a single peck, and also gets drunk after a full bushel."

sentences.zh: 威王曰：「先生飲一斗而醉，惡能飲一石哉！

translations.literal: King Wei said: "If the master gets drunk on one dou, how could he drink a whole dan!
translations.idiomatic: King Wei laughed: "If the master gets drunk after a single peck, how could he possibly drink a whole bushel?

sentences.zh: 其說可得聞乎？」

translations.literal: May I hear the explanation?"
translations.idiomatic: Pray explain this to me."

sentences.zh: 髡曰：「賜酒大王之前，執法在傍，御史在後，髡恐懼俯伏而飲，不過一斗徑醉矣。

translations.literal: Kun said: "When bestowed wine before the Great King, with the law enforcer beside me and the censor behind me, Kun drinks in fear and trembling, prostrate and bowed — after no more than a dou I am quite drunk.
translations.idiomatic: Kun said: "When I am granted the honor of drinking before Your Majesty, with the marshal of law at my side and the imperial censor at my back, I drink in fear and trembling, bowing low to the ground — a single peck is enough to make me drunk.

sentences.zh: 若親有嚴客，髡帣韝鞠跽，待酒於前，時賜餘瀝，奉觴上壽，數起，飲不過二斗徑醉矣。

translations.literal: If my parents have honored guests, Kun tucks up his sleeves and kneels upright, waiting upon them with wine, occasionally receiving the dregs they bestow, raising my cup to toast their health, rising again and again — after no more than two dou I am quite drunk.
translations.idiomatic: When my parents entertain honored guests, I tuck up my sleeves and kneel attentively, pouring wine and waiting upon them. Now and then they bestow the leftover drops, and I raise my cup to toast their health, rising and sitting again and again — two pecks and I am drunk.

sentences.zh: 若朋友交遊，久不相見，卒然相睹，歡然道故，私情相語，飲可五六斗徑醉矣。

translations.literal: If friends and companions who have not seen each other for a long time suddenly meet, joyfully recount old times, and share intimate feelings in conversation, I can drink five or six dou before becoming drunk.
translations.idiomatic: When old friends who have long been parted meet again by chance, joyfully recounting the past and sharing their inmost thoughts — then I can drink five or six pecks before I am drunk.

sentences.zh: 若乃州閭之會，男女雜坐，行酒稽留，六博投壺，相引為曹，握手無罰，目眙不禁，前有墮珥，后有遺簪，髡竊樂此，飲可八斗而醉二參。

translations.literal: If there is a neighborhood gathering where men and women sit mixed together, wine circulates without pause, there are games of liubo and pitch-pot, people pull each other into groups, clasping hands without penalty, staring without prohibition, earrings dropped in front and hairpins lost behind — Kun secretly delights in this, and can drink eight dou, being two-thirds drunk.
translations.idiomatic: At a neighborhood gathering where men and women mingle freely, the wine cups circulate without end, and there are games of dice and pitch-pot — people pull one another into little groups, hands clasp without forfeit, bold glances go unchecked, earrings fall unnoticed to the floor and hairpins slip loose behind — this is where Kun secretly revels, and I can drink eight pecks before I am two-thirds gone.

sentences.zh: 日暮酒闌，合尊促坐，男女同席，履舄交錯，杯盤狼藉，堂上燭滅，主人留髡而送客，羅襦襟解，微聞薌澤，當此之時，髡心最歡，能飲一石。

translations.literal: As evening falls and the wine wanes, cups are combined and seats pressed close, men and women share the same mat, shoes and sandals lie tangled, cups and plates strewn about in disarray, the candles in the hall are extinguished, the host keeps Kun while seeing other guests off, silk jackets fall open at the breast, and a faint fragrance drifts through the air — at such a moment, Kun's heart is most joyful, and I can drink a full dan.
translations.idiomatic: As dusk falls and the feast winds down, the wine vessels are pushed together and the seats drawn close. Men and women share the same mat, shoes and slippers tangled on the floor, cups and dishes scattered in disarray. The candles in the hall gutter out. The host keeps Kun behind while seeing the other guests to the door. Silk jackets fall open at the collar, and the faintest trace of perfume drifts through the air. At such a moment, Kun's heart brims with delight — and I can drink a full bushel.

sentences.zh: 故曰酒極則亂，樂極則悲；

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: when wine reaches its extreme there is disorder, when pleasure reaches its extreme there is sorrow;
translations.idiomatic: And so it is said: when wine goes to excess, chaos follows; when pleasure reaches its height, sorrow awaits.

sentences.zh: 萬事盡然，言不可極，極之而衰。」

translations.literal: All things are the same in this — they say one must not go to extremes, for at the extreme comes decline."
translations.idiomatic: All things under heaven follow this same law — nothing should be pushed to the extreme, for at the extreme comes only decline."

sentences.zh: 以諷諫焉。

translations.literal: By this he offered indirect admonishment.
translations.idiomatic: With this, he delivered his remonstrance through allegory.

sentences.zh: 齊王曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The King of Qi said: "Excellent."
translations.idiomatic: The King of Qi said: "Well spoken."

sentences.zh: 乃罷長夜之飲，以髡為諸侯主客。

translations.literal: He then ceased the all-night drinking and appointed Kun as the master of ceremonies for receiving the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: He put an end to the all-night carousing and appointed Kun as the master of ceremonies for receiving envoys from the feudal lords.

sentences.zh: 宗室置酒，髡嘗在側。

translations.literal: Whenever the royal clan held banquets, Kun was always at his side.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the royal house held a feast, Kun was always at the king's side.

sentences.zh: 其後百餘年，楚有優孟。

translations.literal: More than a hundred years later, Chu had You Meng.
translations.idiomatic: More than a hundred years after this, there arose in Chu a jester called You Meng.

sentences.zh: 優孟，故楚之樂人也。

translations.literal: You Meng was originally a musician of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: You Meng had been an entertainer in the service of Chu.

sentences.zh: 長八尺，多辯，常以談笑諷諫。

translations.literal: He was eight chi tall, highly eloquent, and always used humor and jest to offer indirect admonishment.
translations.idiomatic: He stood eight chi tall, was quick with words, and always delivered his counsel through wit and laughter.

sentences.zh: 楚莊王之時，有所愛馬，衣以文繡，置之華屋之下，席以露床，啗以棗脯。

translations.literal: During the time of King Zhuang of Chu, the king had a beloved horse which he dressed in embroidered silks, housed beneath a magnificent canopy, bedded on an open couch, and fed with dates and dried meat.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhuang of Chu had a horse he doted on. He dressed it in embroidered silks, housed it beneath a splendid canopy, bedded it upon an open couch, and fed it dates and dried meat.

sentences.zh: 馬病肥死，使群臣喪之，欲以棺槨大夫禮葬之。

translations.literal: The horse fell ill from overfeeding and died. The king ordered his ministers to mourn it and wished to bury it in an inner and outer coffin with the rites due a grandee.
translations.idiomatic: The horse grew fat, fell ill, and died. The king commanded his officials to observe mourning for it, and he wished to bury it in a double coffin with the full rites accorded a grandee.

sentences.zh: 左右爭之，以為不可。

translations.literal: Those around him argued against it, considering it improper.
translations.idiomatic: The king's attendants objected, insisting that such a thing was improper.

sentences.zh: 王下令曰：「有敢以馬諫者，罪至死。」

translations.literal: The king issued a decree: "Anyone who dares to remonstrate about the horse shall be punished with death."
translations.idiomatic: The king issued a decree: "Anyone who dares remonstrate with me over the horse shall be put to death."

sentences.zh: 優孟聞之，入殿門。

translations.literal: When You Meng heard this, he entered through the palace gates.
translations.idiomatic: When You Meng heard of this, he strode into the great hall.

sentences.zh: 仰天大哭。

translations.literal: He looked up to the sky and wailed loudly.
translations.idiomatic: He threw back his head and wept aloud to the heavens.

sentences.zh: 王驚而問其故。

translations.literal: The king was startled and asked him the reason.
translations.idiomatic: Startled, the king demanded to know the cause of his grief.

sentences.zh: 優孟曰：「馬者王之所愛也，以楚國堂堂之大，何求不得，而以大夫禮葬之，薄，請以人君禮葬之。」

translations.literal: You Meng said: "The horse was the king's beloved. With the great and mighty state of Chu, what could be unobtainable? Yet to bury it with the rites of a grandee — that is too meager. I beg that it be buried with the rites due a sovereign."
translations.idiomatic: You Meng said: "The horse was Your Majesty's beloved. With all the grandeur and might of Chu at your command, what could be beyond your reach? But to bury it with the rites of a mere grandee — that is too paltry by far! I beg that you bury it with the full rites due a sovereign lord."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「何如？」

translations.literal: The king said: "How so?"
translations.idiomatic: The king asked: "And how would that be done?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣請以彫玉為棺，文梓為槨，楩楓豫章為題湊，發甲卒為穿壙，老弱負土，齊趙陪位於前，韓魏翼衛其后，廟食太牢，奉以萬戶之邑。

translations.literal: He replied: "I request that carved jade be used for the inner coffin, patterned catalpa for the outer coffin, and the finest timber of nanmu, maple, and camphor for the frame. Armored soldiers should be conscripted to dig the tomb, the old and frail to carry the earth. Qi and Zhao shall stand in attendance before it, Han and Wei shall guard its flanks. A temple shall be erected where it is fed the Great Sacrifice, and it shall be granted a fief of ten thousand households."
translations.idiomatic: He replied: "Let the inner coffin be carved of jade, the outer coffin fashioned from figured catalpa, and the frame built of nanmu, maple, and camphor. Conscript armored troops to dig the grave and the old and frail to carry the earth. Let Qi and Zhao stand in attendance before the tomb, with Han and Wei flanking it in guard. Erect a temple where the Great Sacrifice of ox, sheep, and pig is offered, and grant the horse a fief of ten thousand households."

sentences.zh: 諸侯聞之，皆知大王賤人而貴馬也。」

translations.literal: "When the feudal lords hear of this, they will all know that the Great King values horses above men."
translations.idiomatic: "When the feudal lords hear of this, they will all understand that Your Majesty prizes horses above human beings."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「寡人之過一至此乎！

translations.literal: The king said: "Has my fault really gone this far!
translations.idiomatic: The king exclaimed: "Can my folly truly have gone so far!

sentences.zh: 為之柰何？」

translations.literal: What shall be done about it?"
translations.idiomatic: What then should be done?"

sentences.zh: 優孟曰：「請為大王六畜葬之。

translations.literal: You Meng said: "I suggest burying it for the Great King as one would the six domestic animals.
translations.idiomatic: You Meng said: "I suggest Your Majesty bury it as one buries any beast of the barnyard.

sentences.zh: 以壟灶為槨，銅歷為棺，齎以薑棗，薦以木蘭，祭以糧稻，衣以火光，葬之於人腹腸。」

translations.literal: Use an earthen oven as the outer coffin and a bronze cauldron as the inner coffin, season it with ginger and dates, garnish it with magnolia bark, sacrifice to it with rice and grain, dress it with fire, and bury it in the bellies of men."
translations.idiomatic: Let an earthen stove serve as the outer coffin and a bronze cauldron as the inner one. Season it with ginger and dates, garnish it with magnolia bark, make offerings of grain and rice, dress it in robes of flame — and bury it in the bellies of men."

sentences.zh: 於是王乃使以馬屬太官，無令天下久聞也。

translations.literal: Thereupon the king ordered the horse to be handed over to the Grand Provisioner, so that the world would not hear of the matter any longer.
translations.idiomatic: At this, the king had the horse sent to the imperial kitchen, eager that the world should hear no more of the affair.

sentences.zh: 楚相孫叔敖知其賢人也，善待之。

translations.literal: The Chancellor of Chu, Sun Shuao, recognized You Meng as a worthy man and treated him well.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Shuao, the Chancellor of Chu, recognized You Meng as a man of true worth and treated him with great courtesy.

sentences.zh: 病且死，屬其子曰：「我死，汝必貧困。

translations.literal: When Sun Shuao fell ill and was about to die, he charged his son, saying: "When I die, you will surely fall into poverty.
translations.idiomatic: When Sun Shuao lay on his deathbed, he charged his son: "After I am gone, you will surely fall into poverty.

sentences.zh: 若往見優孟，言我孫叔敖之子也。」

translations.literal: Go and see You Meng, and tell him you are the son of Sun Shuao."
translations.idiomatic: When that time comes, go to You Meng and tell him that you are the son of Sun Shuao."

sentences.zh: 居數年，其子窮困負薪，逢優孟，與言曰：「我，孫叔敖子也。

translations.literal: After several years, Sun Shuao's son was reduced to poverty and carrying firewood on his back. He encountered You Meng and spoke to him, saying: "I am the son of Sun Shuao.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later, the son had indeed fallen into destitution and was carrying firewood for a living. He chanced upon You Meng and said: "I am the son of Sun Shuao.

sentences.zh: 父且死時，屬我貧困往見優孟。」

translations.literal: When my father was about to die, he instructed me that in poverty I should go see You Meng."
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed, my father told me that when I fell into poverty, I should seek out You Meng."

sentences.zh: 優孟曰：「若無遠有所之。」

translations.literal: You Meng said: "Do not go far away."
translations.idiomatic: You Meng said: "Stay close by. Do not go wandering off."

sentences.zh: 即為孫叔敖衣冠，抵掌談語。

translations.literal: He then put on the robes and cap of Sun Shuao, and clapped his hands while conversing.
translations.idiomatic: He then donned the robes and cap that Sun Shuao had once worn, mastering the chancellor's gestures and manner of speech.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，像孫叔敖，楚王及左右不能別也。

translations.literal: After more than a year, he so resembled Sun Shuao that the King of Chu and those around him could not tell the difference.
translations.idiomatic: After more than a year of practice, his impersonation of Sun Shuao was so perfect that neither the King of Chu nor his attendants could tell the two apart.

sentences.zh: 莊王置酒，優孟前為壽。

translations.literal: King Zhuang held a banquet, and You Meng came forward to offer a toast.
translations.idiomatic: At a banquet held by King Zhuang, You Meng came forward to offer a toast to the king's health.

sentences.zh: 莊王大驚，以為孫叔敖復生也，欲以為相。

translations.literal: King Zhuang was greatly startled, believing Sun Shuao had come back to life, and wished to make him chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhuang was astonished, convinced that Sun Shuao had returned from the dead. He wanted to appoint him chancellor on the spot.

sentences.zh: 優孟曰：「請歸與婦計之，三日而為相。」

translations.literal: You Meng said: "Allow me to go home and discuss it with my wife, and in three days I will serve as chancellor."
translations.idiomatic: You Meng said: "Allow me to go home and consult with my wife. In three days I shall accept the chancellorship."

sentences.zh: 莊王許之。

translations.literal: King Zhuang granted his request.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhuang agreed.

sentences.zh: 三日後，優孟復來。

translations.literal: Three days later, You Meng returned.
translations.idiomatic: Three days later, You Meng came back.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「婦言謂何？」

translations.literal: The king asked: "What did your wife say?"
translations.idiomatic: The king asked: "And what did your wife have to say?"

sentences.zh: 孟曰：「婦言慎無為，楚相不足為也。

translations.literal: Meng said: "My wife warned me to be careful and not to accept, saying the chancellorship of Chu is not worth holding.
translations.idiomatic: Meng said: "My wife urged me in the strongest terms to decline. She said the chancellorship of Chu is not worth having.

sentences.zh: 如孫叔敖之為楚相，盡忠為廉以治楚，楚王得以霸。

translations.literal: Take Sun Shuao as Chancellor of Chu — he devoted himself utterly to loyalty and integrity in governing Chu, and the King of Chu thereby achieved hegemony.
translations.idiomatic: Consider Sun Shuao as chancellor — he poured out his loyalty and served with perfect integrity, governing Chu so well that the king rose to become hegemon.

sentences.zh: 今死，其子無立錐之地，貧困負薪以自飲食。

translations.literal: Now that he is dead, his son has not a pin's breadth of land to stand on, reduced to poverty and carrying firewood to feed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Now that he is dead, his son has not a pin's breadth of land to stand on. He carries firewood on his back just to keep from starving.

sentences.zh: 必如孫叔敖，不如自殺。」

translations.literal: If one must end up like Sun Shuao, it would be better to kill oneself."
translations.idiomatic: If being chancellor means ending up like Sun Shuao, one might as well take one's own life."

sentences.zh: 因歌曰：「山居耕田苦，難以得食。

translations.literal: He then sang: "Living in the mountains and plowing the fields is bitter toil, and it is hard to get food.
translations.idiomatic: Then he sang: "To dwell in the hills and plow the fields is bitter toil — hard even to keep body and soul together.

sentences.zh: 起而為吏，身貪鄙者餘財，不顧恥辱。

translations.literal: If one rises up to become an official, those who are greedy and base gain surplus wealth and heed no shame or disgrace.
translations.idiomatic: Rise to become an official, and the greedy and base among them grow rich, caring nothing for shame or disgrace.

sentences.zh: 身死家室富，又恐受賕枉法，為姦觸大罪，身死而家滅。

translations.literal: They die with their households wealthy, yet one must also fear accepting bribes and bending the law, committing villainy and incurring great crimes, dying with one's entire family destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: They die with their families wealthy — yet there is always the fear of taking bribes and perverting justice, of committing a crime so grave that one dies and one's whole household is destroyed.

sentences.zh: 貪吏安可為也！

translations.literal: How can one be a corrupt official!
translations.idiomatic: How can anyone bear to be a corrupt official!

sentences.zh: 念為廉吏，奉法守職，竟死不敢為非。

translations.literal: Consider being an honest official, upholding the law and fulfilling one's duties, dying at last without ever daring to do wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Think instead of being an honest official — upholding the law, fulfilling one's duties, never once daring to do wrong until the very day of one's death.

sentences.zh: 廉吏安可為也！

translations.literal: How can one be an honest official!
translations.idiomatic: But how can anyone bear to be an honest official either!

sentences.zh: 楚相孫叔敖持廉至死，方今妻子窮困負薪而食，不足為也！」

translations.literal: The Chancellor of Chu, Sun Shuao, maintained his integrity until death, yet now his wife and children are reduced to poverty, carrying firewood for food — it is not worth doing!"
translations.idiomatic: Sun Shuao, Chancellor of Chu, held fast to his integrity unto death — yet today his wife and children carry firewood on their backs just to eat. It is not worth doing!"

sentences.zh: 於是莊王謝優孟，乃召孫叔敖子，封之寢丘四百戶，以奉其祀。

translations.literal: Thereupon King Zhuang thanked You Meng, summoned the son of Sun Shuao, and enfeoffed him with four hundred households at Qinqiu to maintain the family sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: At this, King Zhuang apologized to You Meng. He summoned the son of Sun Shuao and enfeoffed him with four hundred households at Qinqiu, so that the ancestral sacrifices might be maintained.

sentences.zh: 后十世不絕。

translations.literal: For ten generations afterward, the line was not cut off.
translations.idiomatic: The family line continued unbroken for ten generations.

sentences.zh: 此知可以言時矣。

translations.literal: This shows the wisdom of knowing when to speak.
translations.idiomatic: This was a man who understood the art of speaking at the right moment.

sentences.zh: 其後二百餘年，秦有優旃。

translations.literal: More than two hundred years later, Qin had You Zhan.
translations.idiomatic: More than two hundred years after this, there arose in Qin a jester called You Zhan.

sentences.zh: 優旃者，秦倡侏儒也。

translations.literal: You Zhan was an entertainer and dwarf of Qin.
translations.idiomatic: You Zhan was a dwarf entertainer in the court of Qin.

sentences.zh: 善為笑言，然合於大道，秦始皇時，置酒而天雨，陛楯者皆沾寒。

translations.literal: He was skilled at making jests, yet they always accorded with the great Way. During the time of the First Emperor of Qin, a banquet was held while it rained from the sky, and the palace guards standing on the steps were all drenched and cold.
translations.idiomatic: He was a master of witty remarks, yet his jests always struck at the greater truth. During the reign of the First Emperor, a banquet was being held when rain began to fall, and the guards standing watch on the palace steps were soaked through and shivering.

sentences.zh: 優旃見而哀之，謂之曰：「汝欲休乎？」

translations.literal: You Zhan saw them and pitied them, and said to them: "Do you want to rest?"
translations.idiomatic: You Zhan saw their misery and felt pity for them. He called out: "Would you like a rest?"

sentences.zh: 陛楯者皆曰：「幸甚。」

translations.literal: The guards all said: "That would be most fortunate."
translations.idiomatic: The guards all cried: "We would be most grateful!"

sentences.zh: 優旃曰：「我即呼汝，汝疾應曰諾。」

translations.literal: You Zhan said: "When I call out to you, answer 'Yes!' at once."
translations.idiomatic: You Zhan said: "When I call out to you, answer 'Aye!' as quick as you can."

sentences.zh: 居有頃，殿上上壽呼萬歲。

translations.literal: After a short time, a toast was raised in the hall and the cry of "Ten thousand years!" went up.
translations.idiomatic: Before long, a toast was raised in the great hall and the cry of "Ten thousand years!" rang out.

sentences.zh: 優旃臨檻大呼曰：「陛楯郎！」

translations.literal: You Zhan leaned over the railing and shouted: "Palace step guards!"
translations.idiomatic: You Zhan leaned over the balustrade and bellowed: "Gentlemen of the guard!"

sentences.zh: 郎曰：「諾。」

translations.literal: The guards answered: "Yes!"
translations.idiomatic: The guards answered as one: "Aye!"

sentences.zh: 優旃曰：「汝雖長，何益，幸雨立。

translations.literal: You Zhan said: "Though you are tall, what good does it do you? You are fortunate enough to stand in the rain.
translations.idiomatic: You Zhan called out: "Tall as you are, what good does it do you? You get the privilege of standing in the rain.

sentences.zh: 我雖短也，幸休居。」

translations.literal: Though I am short, I am fortunate enough to sit and rest."
translations.idiomatic: Short as I am, I get the privilege of sitting inside where it is dry."

sentences.zh: 於是始皇使陛楯者得半相代。

translations.literal: Thereupon the First Emperor allowed the guards to take turns in shifts, with half relieved at a time.
translations.idiomatic: At this, the First Emperor ordered that the guards be allowed to take turns, half on duty and half resting.

sentences.zh: 始皇嘗議欲大苑囿，東至函谷關，西至雍、陳倉。

translations.literal: The First Emperor once proposed to greatly expand the imperial park, extending east to Hangu Pass and west to Yong and Chencang.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor once proposed to vastly expand the imperial hunting park, stretching it east to Hangu Pass and west to Yong and Chencang.

sentences.zh: 優旃曰：「善。

translations.literal: You Zhan said: "Excellent.
translations.idiomatic: You Zhan said: "A splendid idea!

sentences.zh: 多縱禽獸於其中，寇從東方來，令麋鹿觸之足矣。」

translations.literal: Stock it with plenty of wild animals, and when invaders come from the east, just have the elk charge at them — that will be enough."
translations.idiomatic: Fill it with game animals aplenty, and when the invaders come from the east, simply order the elk to charge them — that should be quite sufficient."

sentences.zh: 始皇以故輟止。

translations.literal: The First Emperor therefore abandoned the plan.
translations.idiomatic: Because of this, the First Emperor abandoned the plan.

sentences.zh: 二世立，又欲漆其城。

translations.literal: When the Second Emperor came to the throne, he wanted to lacquer the city walls.
translations.idiomatic: When the Second Emperor ascended the throne, he conceived the notion of lacquering the city walls.

sentences.zh: 優旃曰：「善。

translations.literal: You Zhan said: "Excellent.
translations.idiomatic: You Zhan said: "A wonderful idea!

sentences.zh: 主上雖無言，臣固將請之。

translations.literal: Even if Your Majesty had not spoken, your servant was already going to propose it.
translations.idiomatic: Even had Your Majesty not proposed it, your servant was just about to suggest it himself.

sentences.zh: 漆城雖於百姓愁費，然佳哉！

translations.literal: Although lacquering the walls would cause the people grief and expense, how magnificent it would be!
translations.idiomatic: True, lacquering the walls will burden the people with grief and expense — but what a magnificent sight it will be!

sentences.zh: 漆城蕩蕩，寇來不能上。

translations.literal: The lacquered walls will be smooth and gleaming — when invaders come, they will not be able to climb up.
translations.idiomatic: The lacquered walls will gleam so smooth and slippery that when invaders come, they will never be able to scale them.

sentences.zh: 即欲就之，易為漆耳，顧難為蔭室。」

translations.literal: If one wishes to carry this out, the lacquering itself is easy, but the hard part would be building a drying shed big enough to cover the walls."
translations.idiomatic: The only difficulty is this: applying the lacquer is easy enough, but where on earth would we build a drying shed large enough to cover the walls while they cure?"

sentences.zh: 於是二世笑之，以其故止。

translations.literal: The Second Emperor laughed at this and abandoned the plan for this reason.
translations.idiomatic: The Second Emperor burst out laughing and dropped the idea.

sentences.zh: 居無何，二世殺死，優旃歸漢，數年而卒。

translations.literal: Not long after, the Second Emperor was killed. You Zhan submitted to Han, and after several years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Not long after, the Second Emperor was killed. You Zhan went over to the Han dynasty, and some years later he passed away.

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：淳于髡仰天大笑，齊威王橫行。

translations.literal: The Grand Historian says: Chunyu Kun threw back his head and laughed at the sky, and King Wei of Qi swept all before him.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: Chunyu Kun threw back his head and laughed to the heavens, and King Wei of Qi marched unchallenged across the land.

sentences.zh: 優孟搖頭而歌，負薪者以封。

translations.literal: You Meng shook his head and sang, and the firewood carrier was enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: You Meng shook his head and broke into song, and the man who carried firewood received a fief.

sentences.zh: 優旃臨檻疾呼，陛楯得以半更。

translations.literal: You Zhan leaned over the railing and cried out, and the palace guards were allowed to take half-shifts.
translations.idiomatic: You Zhan leaned over the balustrade and cried out, and the guards were granted the mercy of half-shifts.

sentences.zh: 豈不亦偉哉！

translations.literal: Is this not also magnificent!
translations.idiomatic: Were these men not also magnificent in their way!

sentences.zh: 褚先生曰：臣幸得以經術為郎，而好讀外家傳語。

translations.literal: Master Chu says: Your servant was fortunate to be appointed a palace gentleman through his study of the Classics, and he has loved reading the traditions and stories of the various schools.
translations.idiomatic: Master Chu says: Your servant was fortunate enough to gain his position as a court gentleman through scholarship in the Classics, and he has always delighted in reading the traditions and tales of the various schools.

sentences.zh: 竊不遜讓，復作故事滑稽之語六章，編之於左。

translations.literal: Presumptuously and without modesty, I have composed six additional chapters of anecdotes and jests, appended below.
translations.idiomatic: With more boldness than modesty, I have composed six additional tales of wit and humor, which are appended below.

sentences.zh: 可以覽觀揚意，以示後世好事者讀之，以游心駭耳，以附益上方太史公之三章。

translations.literal: These may be browsed and contemplated to elevate the mind, shown to curious readers of later generations for the delight of the imagination and the startling of the ears, appended as a supplement to the three chapters of the Grand Historian above.
translations.idiomatic: These may be perused for amusement and edification, and offered to curious readers of later ages for the pleasure of the mind and the surprise of the ear — appended here as a supplement to the Grand Historian's three tales above.

sentences.zh: 武帝時有所幸倡郭舍人者，發言陳辭雖不合大道，然令人主和說。

translations.literal: During the time of Emperor Wu, there was a favored entertainer named Guo Sheren, whose speech and arguments, though they did not accord with the great Way, nevertheless pleased and amused the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: In the time of Emperor Wu, there was a favored jester by the name of Guo Sheren. His words and quips may not have accorded with the great Way, but they never failed to put the emperor in good humor.

sentences.zh: 武帝少時，東武侯母常養帝，帝壯時，號之曰「大乳母」。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu was young, the mother of the Marquis of Dongwu had often nursed him. When the emperor grew up, he called her the 'Great Wet Nurse.'
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Wu was a boy, the mother of the Marquis of Dongwu had served as his wet nurse. When he grew to manhood, he honored her with the title 'the Great Nurse.'

sentences.zh: 率一月再朝。

translations.literal: On average, she had audience at court twice a month.
translations.idiomatic: She was received at court twice a month as a matter of course.

sentences.zh: 朝奏入，有詔使幸臣馬游卿以帛五十匹賜乳母，又奉飲糒飱養乳母。

translations.literal: When her memorials were presented at court, there were edicts ordering the favored minister Ma Youqing to bestow fifty bolts of silk upon the wet nurse, and also to provide her with food and drink and provisions to support her.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever she submitted a memorial, the emperor would order his favored minister Ma Youqing to bestow fifty bolts of silk upon the nurse, along with food and provisions for her maintenance.

sentences.zh: 乳母上書曰：「某所有公田，願得假倩之。」

translations.literal: The wet nurse submitted a petition saying: "There are public fields at such-and-such a place; I wish to be allowed to lease them."
translations.idiomatic: The nurse petitioned the throne: "There are public fields at a certain place. I beg to be allowed to lease them."

sentences.zh: 帝曰：「乳母欲得之乎？」

translations.literal: The emperor said: "The nurse wishes to have them?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said: "The nurse wants them?"

sentences.zh: 以賜乳母。

translations.literal: He bestowed them upon the nurse.
translations.idiomatic: And he granted them to her.

sentences.zh: 乳母所言，未嘗不聽。

translations.literal: Whatever the nurse asked for, the emperor never failed to comply.
translations.idiomatic: Whatever the nurse requested, the emperor never refused.

sentences.zh: 有詔得令乳母乘車行馳道中。

translations.literal: An edict was issued permitting the nurse to ride in a carriage on the imperial roads.
translations.idiomatic: A special edict was issued granting the nurse the privilege of traveling by carriage on the imperial thoroughfare.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，公卿大臣皆敬重乳母。

translations.literal: At this time, the dukes, ministers, and great officials all treated the nurse with respect and deference.
translations.idiomatic: At this time, every minister and high official in the court treated the nurse with the greatest respect.

sentences.zh: 乳母家子孫奴從者橫暴長安中，當道掣頓人車馬，奪人衣服。

translations.literal: The children, grandchildren, slaves, and servants of the nurse's household acted with violent arrogance throughout Chang'an, seizing people's carriages and horses in the middle of the road and snatching their clothes.
translations.idiomatic: The nurse's children, grandchildren, and household servants ran rampant through Chang'an. They blocked the roads, dragged people from their carts, and stripped the clothes from their backs.

sentences.zh: 聞於中，不忍致之法。

translations.literal: When the emperor heard of this at court, he could not bear to subject her to the law.
translations.idiomatic: When word of this reached the emperor, he could not bring himself to punish her.

sentences.zh: 有司請徙乳母家室，處之於邊。

translations.literal: The responsible officials petitioned to have the nurse's entire household relocated to the frontier.
translations.idiomatic: The responsible officials petitioned to have the nurse's entire household banished to the frontier.

sentences.zh: 奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The petition was approved.

sentences.zh: 乳母當入至前，面見辭。

translations.literal: The nurse was to enter the palace and come before the emperor to bid farewell in person.
translations.idiomatic: The nurse was to present herself before the emperor for a final audience to take her leave.

sentences.zh: 乳母先見郭舍人，為下泣。

translations.literal: The nurse first went to see Guo Sheren, weeping before him.
translations.idiomatic: Before the audience, the nurse went to see Guo Sheren and wept before him.

sentences.zh: 舍人曰：「即入見辭去，疾步數還顧。」

translations.literal: Sheren said: "When you go in to take your leave, walk away quickly but keep turning back to look."
translations.idiomatic: Sheren told her: "When you go in to bid farewell, walk away briskly — but keep glancing back over your shoulder."

sentences.zh: 乳母如其言，謝去，疾步數還顧。

translations.literal: The nurse did as he said: she bid farewell and departed, walking quickly and turning back to look several times.
translations.idiomatic: The nurse followed his instructions. She said her farewell and turned to leave, walking briskly but glancing back again and again.

sentences.zh: 郭舍人疾言罵之曰：「咄！

translations.literal: Guo Sheren shouted at her in a scolding tone: "Bah!
translations.idiomatic: Guo Sheren barked after her: "Bah!

sentences.zh: 老女子！

translations.literal: Old woman!
translations.idiomatic: You foolish old woman!

sentences.zh: 何不疾行！

translations.literal: Why don't you hurry along!
translations.idiomatic: Get moving, won't you!

sentences.zh: 陛下已壯矣，寧尚須汝乳而活邪？

translations.literal: His Majesty is a grown man now — does he still need your milk to live?
translations.idiomatic: His Majesty is a grown man now — does he still need your breast to survive?

sentences.zh: 尚何還顧！」

translations.literal: What are you still looking back for!"
translations.idiomatic: What is there to keep looking back for!"

sentences.zh: 於是人主憐焉悲之，乃下詔止無徙乳母，罰謫譖之者。

translations.literal: At this, the emperor was moved to pity and sorrow for her, and issued an edict rescinding the banishment of the nurse and punishing those who had slandered her.
translations.idiomatic: At this, the emperor was overcome with pity and grief. He issued an edict revoking the nurse's banishment and punished those who had spoken against her.

sentences.zh: 武帝時，齊人有東方生名朔，以好古傳書，愛經術，多所博觀外家之語。

translations.literal: During the time of Emperor Wu, there was a man from Qi surnamed Dongfang and named Shuo, who loved the ancient traditions and writings, cherished the study of the Classics, and read widely in the works of the various schools.
translations.idiomatic: In the time of Emperor Wu, there was a man from Qi surnamed Dongfang, whose given name was Shuo. He was devoted to the ancient traditions, loved the study of the Classics, and had read widely in the writings of the various schools.

sentences.zh: 朔初入長安，至公車上書，凡用三千奏牘。

translations.literal: When Shuo first came to Chang'an, he submitted a memorial at the Office of the Public Carriage, using a total of three thousand bamboo writing tablets.
translations.idiomatic: When Shuo first arrived in Chang'an, he submitted a memorial through the Office of the Public Carriage. It filled three thousand bamboo tablets.

sentences.zh: 公車令兩人共持舉其書，僅然能勝之。

translations.literal: The Director of the Public Carriage needed two men working together to hold and lift his document, and they could barely manage it.
translations.idiomatic: It took two men from the Office of the Public Carriage just to lift the memorial, and even then they could barely carry it.

sentences.zh: 人主從上方讀之，止，輒乙其處，讀之二月乃盡。

translations.literal: The emperor read it from beginning to end; whenever he stopped, he would mark his place with a notch, and it took him two months to finish.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read it through from beginning to end, marking his place with a notch each time he paused. It took him two full months to finish.

sentences.zh: 詔拜以為郎，常在側侍中。

translations.literal: An edict was issued appointing him as a palace gentleman, and he was always at the emperor's side in attendance.
translations.idiomatic: By imperial edict, Shuo was appointed a palace gentleman and kept always in attendance at the emperor's side.

sentences.zh: 數召至前談語，人主未嘗不說也。

translations.literal: He was frequently summoned before the emperor to talk and converse, and the emperor was never anything but pleased.
translations.idiomatic: He was frequently called before the emperor for conversation, and the emperor never failed to be delighted.

sentences.zh: 時詔賜之食於前。

translations.literal: From time to time the emperor would order food to be served to him in the imperial presence.
translations.idiomatic: From time to time, the emperor ordered food to be served to him right there at court.

sentences.zh: 飯已，盡懷其餘肉持去，衣盡汙。

translations.literal: After eating, he would stuff all the remaining meat into his clothes and carry it away, staining his garments completely.
translations.idiomatic: When the meal was done, he would stuff every last scrap of leftover meat into the folds of his robe and carry it off, leaving his clothes thoroughly stained with grease.

sentences.zh: 數賜縑帛，檐揭而去。

translations.literal: He was frequently given gifts of silk, which he would carry off draped over his shoulders.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the emperor bestowed bolts of silk upon him, he would sling them over his shoulder and stride away.

sentences.zh: 徒用所賜錢帛，取少婦於長安中好女。

translations.literal: He spent all the money and silk he was given on taking young wives from among the beautiful women of Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: He used every coin and bolt of silk he received to take young wives from among the most beautiful women of Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 率取婦一歲所者即棄去，更取婦。

translations.literal: As a rule, he would keep a wife for about a year and then abandon her, taking a new wife.
translations.idiomatic: He would keep each wife for about a year before casting her aside and marrying a new one.

sentences.zh: 所賜錢財盡索之於女子。

translations.literal: All the money and wealth he was given was spent entirely on women.
translations.idiomatic: Every bit of wealth the emperor bestowed on him was lavished upon women.

sentences.zh: 人主左右諸郎半呼之「狂人」。

translations.literal: Half of the palace gentlemen around the emperor called him 'the madman.'
translations.idiomatic: Half the gentlemen at court took to calling him 'the Madman.'

sentences.zh: 人主聞之，曰：「令朔在事無為是行者，若等安能及之哉！」

translations.literal: When the emperor heard this, he said: "If Shuo conducted his affairs without engaging in such behavior, how could the likes of you ever match him!"
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor heard this, he said: "If Shuo held his office without indulging in such antics, how could the likes of you ever hope to match him!"

sentences.zh: 朔任其子為郎，又為侍謁者，常持節出使。

translations.literal: Shuo had his son appointed as a palace gentleman, and he himself also served as an attendant and reception officer, frequently carrying the imperial tally on diplomatic missions.
translations.idiomatic: Shuo had his son appointed a palace gentleman. He himself also served as an attendant and reception officer, and was frequently sent on missions bearing the imperial tally.

sentences.zh: 朔行殿中，郎謂之曰：「人皆以先生為狂。」

translations.literal: As Shuo was walking through the palace hall, a gentleman said to him: "Everyone considers the master to be mad."
translations.idiomatic: As Shuo was passing through the palace hall one day, a gentleman said to him: "Everyone thinks you are mad, sir."

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「如朔等，所謂避世於朝廷閒者也。

translations.literal: Shuo said: "Men like Shuo are what is called those who withdraw from the world within the court itself.
translations.idiomatic: Shuo replied: "Men like me are what you might call recluses who hide from the world right here at court.

sentences.zh: 古之人，乃避世於深山中。」

translations.literal: The men of old withdrew from the world into the deep mountains."
translations.idiomatic: The recluses of old fled into the deep mountains."

sentences.zh: 時坐席中，酒酣，據地歌曰：「陸沈於俗，避世金馬門。

translations.literal: Once, sitting among the guests when the wine was flowing freely, he slapped the ground and sang: "Sinking into obscurity among the common world, I withdraw from the world at the Gate of the Golden Horse.
translations.idiomatic: Once, deep into his cups at a banquet, he slapped the ground and sang: "Sunk in obscurity among the vulgar crowd, I hide from the world at the Gate of the Golden Horse.

sentences.zh: 宮殿中可以避世全身，何必深山之中，蒿廬之下。」

translations.literal: Within the palace one can withdraw from the world and preserve one's life — why must one go to the deep mountains and dwell in a thatched hut?"
translations.idiomatic: Within these palace walls a man can hide from the world and keep himself whole — what need is there for deep mountains and thatched huts?"

sentences.zh: 金馬門者，宦［者］署門也，門傍有銅馬，故謂之曰「金馬門」。

translations.literal: The Gate of the Golden Horse was the gate of the office of the palace attendants. Beside the gate there was a bronze horse, which is why it was called the 'Gate of the Golden Horse.'
translations.idiomatic: The Gate of the Golden Horse was the entrance to the offices of the palace attendants. A bronze horse stood beside the gate, which is how it got its name.

sentences.zh: 時會聚宮下博士諸先生與論議，共難之曰：「蘇秦、張儀一當萬乘之主，而都卿相之位，澤及後世。

translations.literal: Once, the court scholars and academicians gathered and debated with him, challenging him together: "Su Qin and Zhang Yi each faced the rulers of ten-thousand-chariot states, rose to the positions of minister and chancellor, and their benefits reached to later generations.
translations.idiomatic: Once, the court academicians and scholars assembled and challenged him in debate: "Su Qin and Zhang Yi each won audience with the mightiest rulers of their age, rose to the rank of minister and chancellor, and their influence was felt for generations to come.

sentences.zh: 今子大夫修先王之術，慕聖人之義，諷誦詩書百家之言，不可勝數。

translations.literal: Now you, sir, have studied the arts of the former kings, admired the righteous ways of the sages, and recited the Odes, the Documents, and the words of the hundred schools — too numerous to count.
translations.idiomatic: Now you, sir, have cultivated the arts of the former kings, revered the righteous ways of the sages, and committed to memory the Odes, the Documents, and the writings of the hundred schools — more than can be counted.

sentences.zh: 著於竹帛，自以為海內無雙，即可謂博聞辯智矣。

translations.literal: Your writings fill bamboo and silk, and you consider yourself without equal in the realm — one might indeed call you broadly learned and eloquently wise.
translations.idiomatic: Your writings fill scroll after scroll, and you consider yourself without peer in all the realm — a man of vast learning and eloquent wit, by any measure.

sentences.zh: 然悉力盡忠以事聖帝，曠日持久，積數十年，官不過侍郎，位不過執戟，意者尚有遺行邪？

translations.literal: Yet despite exhausting your strength and devotion in serving the sage emperor, over the long course of decades, your office has never risen above Attendant Gentleman, your rank never beyond Halberd-Bearer. Could there perhaps still be some fault in your conduct?
translations.idiomatic: And yet, for all your exhausting loyalty in the emperor's service, over the course of decades your office has never risen above Attendant Gentleman and your rank has never surpassed Halberd-Bearer. Could it be that there is something wanting in your conduct?

sentences.zh: 其故何也？」

translations.literal: What is the reason for this?"
translations.idiomatic: What is the reason for this?"

sentences.zh: 東方生曰：「是固非子所能備也。

translations.literal: Master Dongfang said: "This is something you are simply unable to comprehend.
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo replied: "This is something that lies entirely beyond your understanding.

sentences.zh: 彼一時也，此一時也，豈可同哉！

translations.literal: That was one era, and this is another — how can they be compared!
translations.idiomatic: Those were different times, and these are different times — how can the two be compared!

sentences.zh: 夫張儀、蘇秦之時，周室大壞，諸侯不朝，力政爭權，相禽以兵，并為十二國，未有雌雄，得士者彊，失士者亡，故說聽行通，身處尊位，澤及後世，子孫長榮。

translations.literal: In the time of Zhang Yi and Su Qin, the house of Zhou was in great ruin, the feudal lords no longer attended court, they governed by force and contended for power, seizing one another through warfare, merged into twelve states with no clear victor — those who won scholars grew strong, those who lost them perished. Therefore their counsel was heeded, their proposals accepted, they occupied the highest positions, their influence reached later generations, and their descendants prospered for ages.
translations.idiomatic: In the days of Zhang Yi and Su Qin, the house of Zhou lay in ruins. The feudal lords no longer came to court; they governed by force and vied for supremacy, waging war upon one another. The world was divided among a dozen states, with no clear victor. Those who won talented men grew strong; those who lost them perished. And so these men's counsel was heeded, their plans put into action. They rose to the highest offices, their influence endured for generations, and their descendants prospered long after them.

sentences.zh: 今非然也。

translations.literal: Now it is not so.
translations.idiomatic: Today, things are entirely different.

sentences.zh: 聖帝在上，德流天下，諸侯賓服，威振四夷，連四海之外以為席，安於覆盂，天下平均，合為一家，動發舉事，猶如運之掌中。

translations.literal: A sage emperor sits on the throne, his virtue flows throughout the realm, the feudal lords submit as guests, his authority shakes the four barbarian peoples, the lands beyond the four seas are spread like a mat, as stable as an overturned bowl, the empire is level and equal, united as one family, and every undertaking he sets in motion is as easy as turning something in the palm of his hand.
translations.idiomatic: A sage emperor reigns above, and his virtue flows to every corner of the realm. The feudal lords submit in deference, his authority makes the barbarians of the four directions tremble. The lands beyond the four seas lie before him like a spread mat, as steady as an overturned bowl. All under heaven is unified and at peace, joined as one great family, and every enterprise the emperor undertakes is as effortless as turning an object in the palm of his hand.

sentences.zh: 賢與不肖，何以異哉？

translations.literal: Between the worthy and the unworthy, what difference is there?
translations.idiomatic: In such an age, what difference is there between the worthy and the unworthy?

sentences.zh: 方今以天下之大，士民之眾，竭精馳說，并進輻湊者，不可勝數。

translations.literal: At present, given the vastness of the empire and the multitude of scholars and common people, those who exhaust their talents and rush about peddling their ideas, pressing forward and converging from all sides, cannot be counted.
translations.idiomatic: Now, given the vastness of the empire and the sheer multitude of its people, those who pour out their talents and hawk their ideas, pressing forward from every direction — they are beyond counting.

sentences.zh: 悉力慕義，困於衣食，或失門戶。

translations.literal: They devote all their strength to pursuing righteousness, yet are reduced to poverty in food and clothing, and some even lose their family standing.
translations.idiomatic: They devote every ounce of their strength to the pursuit of virtue, yet they can scarcely clothe and feed themselves, and some have lost even their family standing.

sentences.zh: 使張儀、蘇秦與仆并生於今之世，曾不能得掌故，安敢望常侍侍郎乎！

translations.literal: If Zhang Yi and Su Qin were born together with me in this present age, they could not even obtain the post of a minor archivist — how would they dare hope for the rank of Regular Attendant or Attendant Gentleman!
translations.idiomatic: If Zhang Yi and Su Qin were alive today, born into this same age as I, they could not so much as land a post as a petty archivist — how then would they dare dream of becoming Regular Attendant or Attendant Gentleman!

sentences.zh: 傳曰：『天下無害菑，雖有聖人，無所施其才；

translations.literal: The tradition says: 'When the world is free from harm and disaster, though there be sages, they have no occasion to exercise their talents;
translations.idiomatic: As the tradition has it: 'When the world is free of calamity and ruin, even a sage has no occasion to display his talents.

sentences.zh: 上下和同，雖有賢者，無所立功。』

translations.literal: When high and low are harmonious and united, though there be worthy men, they have no occasion to establish merit.'
translations.idiomatic: When those above and below are in harmony, even a worthy man has no occasion to win distinction.'

sentences.zh: 故曰時異則事異。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: when the times change, affairs change with them.
translations.idiomatic: And so it is said: different times call for different measures.

sentences.zh: 雖然，安可以不務修身乎？

translations.literal: Even so, how can one fail to devote oneself to self-cultivation?
translations.idiomatic: And yet, how can a man neglect the cultivation of his own character?

sentences.zh: 《詩》曰：『鼓鐘于宮，聲聞于外。

translations.literal: The Odes say: 'When drums and bells sound in the palace, their sound is heard outside.
translations.idiomatic: As the Odes say: 'When drums and bells sound within the palace, their music carries beyond the walls.

sentences.zh: 鶴鳴九皋，聲聞于天。』

translations.literal: The crane cries at the ninth marsh, and its voice is heard in heaven.'
translations.idiomatic: The crane cries from the deepest marsh, and its voice reaches the heavens.'

translations.literal: [Punctuation mark only]
translations.idiomatic: [End of quotation]

sentences.zh: 茍能修身，何患不榮！

translations.literal: If one can cultivate oneself, why worry about not being honored!
translations.idiomatic: If a man can truly cultivate himself, what need has he to worry that recognition will not come!

sentences.zh: 太公躬行仁義七十二年，逢文王，得行其說，封於齊，七百歲而不絕。

translations.literal: The Grand Duke personally practiced benevolence and righteousness for seventy-two years before he met King Wen, was able to put his ideas into action, and was enfeoffed in Qi, where his line endured for seven hundred years without interruption.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Duke practiced benevolence and righteousness in person for seventy-two years before he met King Wen, gained the chance to put his ideas into practice, and was enfeoffed in Qi — where his line endured unbroken for seven hundred years.

sentences.zh: 此士之所以日夜孜孜，修學行道，不敢止也。

translations.literal: This is why scholars toil day and night without rest, studying and walking the Way, never daring to stop.
translations.idiomatic: This is why the scholar toils day and night without respite, cultivating his learning and walking the Way, never daring to cease.

sentences.zh: 今世之處士，時雖不用，崛然獨立，塊然獨處，上觀許由，下察接輿，策同范蠡，忠合子胥，天下和平，與義相扶，寡偶少徒，固其常也。

translations.literal: The recluse scholars of our age, though the times do not employ them, stand towering and alone, dwelling in solitary grandeur. They look up to Xu You, study Jie Yu below, their strategy matches Fan Li, their loyalty accords with Zixu. The world is at peace, and they support each other with righteousness. Having few companions and fewer followers — this is simply their normal lot.
translations.idiomatic: The recluse scholars of our age, though the times have no use for them, stand tall and solitary, dwelling apart in their grandeur. They look upward to Xu You for inspiration and downward to Jie Yu; their strategems rival those of Fan Li, and their loyalty matches that of Wu Zixu. The world is at peace, and they sustain themselves through righteousness. That they have few companions and fewer followers — this is simply their lot.

sentences.zh: 子何疑於余哉！」

translations.literal: Why should you doubt me!"
translations.idiomatic: Why then do you question me!"

sentences.zh: 於是諸先生默然無以應也。

translations.literal: At this, all the scholars fell silent, having nothing to say in reply.
translations.idiomatic: At this, every one of the scholars fell silent, with not a word to say in reply.

sentences.zh: 建章宮後閤重櫟中有物出焉，其狀似麋。

translations.literal: In the rear courtyard of the Jianzhang Palace, among a thicket of oaks, a creature appeared whose shape resembled an elk.
translations.idiomatic: In the rear gallery of the Jianzhang Palace, amid a dense stand of oaks, a strange creature appeared. Its form resembled that of an elk.

sentences.zh: 以聞，武帝往臨視之。

translations.literal: When the report was made, Emperor Wu went in person to look at it.
translations.idiomatic: When word reached the throne, Emperor Wu went in person to inspect it.

sentences.zh: 問左右群臣習事通經術者，莫能知。

translations.literal: He asked those around him, the ministers who were versed in affairs and learned in the Classics, but none could identify it.
translations.idiomatic: He questioned the ministers around him — those versed in affairs and learned in the Classics — but none of them could identify it.

sentences.zh: 詔東方朔視之。

translations.literal: An edict was issued for Dongfang Shuo to come examine it.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered Dongfang Shuo to come and have a look.

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「臣知之，願賜美酒粱飯大飱臣，臣乃言。」

translations.literal: Shuo said: "Your servant knows what it is. I beg that you bestow fine wine and millet rice upon your servant as a great feast, and then I will speak."
translations.idiomatic: Shuo said: "Your servant knows what it is. But first, I beg that Your Majesty reward me with fine wine and a feast of the best millet — and only then will I speak."

sentences.zh: 詔曰：「可。」

translations.literal: The edict said: "Granted."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said: "Granted."

sentences.zh: 已又曰：「某所有公田魚池蒲葦數頃，陛下以賜臣，臣朔乃言。」

translations.literal: After this he further said: "There are public fields with fish ponds and beds of rushes and reeds at such-and-such a place, several qing in area. I beg Your Majesty to bestow them upon your servant, and then I, Shuo, will speak."
translations.idiomatic: After the feast, Shuo added: "At a certain place there are public fields with fish ponds and beds of rushes and reeds — several qing in area. If Your Majesty would bestow them upon me, then I shall tell you what the creature is."

sentences.zh: 詔曰：「可。」

translations.literal: The edict said: "Granted."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor replied: "Granted."

sentences.zh: 於是朔乃肯言，曰：「所謂騶牙者也。

translations.literal: Only then did Shuo consent to speak, saying: "It is what is called a zouya.
translations.idiomatic: Only then did Shuo consent to speak. He said: "This is what is known as a zouya.

sentences.zh: 遠方當來歸義，而騶牙先見。

translations.literal: When distant peoples are about to come and submit to righteousness, the zouya appears beforehand.
translations.idiomatic: When people from distant lands are about to come and submit in allegiance, the zouya appears as a harbinger.

sentences.zh: 其齒前后若一，齊等無牙，故謂之騶牙。」

translations.literal: Its teeth, front and back, are all alike, even and without tusks — therefore it is called the 'zouya' (orderly teeth)."
translations.idiomatic: Its teeth are uniform from front to back, even and without tusks — which is why it is called the zouya, the creature of orderly teeth."

sentences.zh: 其後一歲所，匈奴混邪王果將十萬眾來降漢。

translations.literal: About a year later, the Xiongnu King Hunye did indeed lead a force of a hundred thousand to surrender to Han.
translations.idiomatic: About a year later, just as Shuo had predicted, the Xiongnu King Hunye led a hundred thousand of his people to surrender to the Han.

sentences.zh: 乃復賜東方生錢財甚多。

translations.literal: The emperor once again bestowed upon Dongfang Shuo a great deal of money and gifts.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor once again lavished Dongfang Shuo with generous gifts of money and treasure.

sentences.zh: 至老，朔且死時，諫曰：「《詩》云『營營青蠅，止于蕃。

translations.literal: In his old age, when Shuo was about to die, he admonished the emperor, saying: "The Odes say, 'The buzzing green flies settle on the fence.
translations.idiomatic: In old age, when Shuo lay on his deathbed, he offered a final admonition: "The Odes say, 'The buzzing green flies settle on the fence.

sentences.zh: 愷悌君子，無信讒言。

translations.literal: O gentle and kind lord, do not believe slanderous words.
translations.idiomatic: O kind and gracious lord, give no credit to slanderous words.

sentences.zh: 讒言罔極，交亂四國』。

translations.literal: Slanderous words know no limit, and bring turmoil to the four quarters.'
translations.idiomatic: Slander knows no bounds, and throws the four quarters of the realm into confusion.'

sentences.zh: 願陛下遠巧佞，退讒言。」

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty to keep far from the cunning and the sycophantic, and to reject slanderous speech."
translations.idiomatic: I beg Your Majesty to keep your distance from the cunning and the sycophantic, and to cast aside all slanderous counsel."

sentences.zh: 帝曰：「今顧東方朔多善言？」

translations.literal: The emperor said: "How is it that Dongfang Shuo now speaks so many good words?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said: "Since when has Dongfang Shuo had so many wise things to say?"

sentences.zh: 怪之。

translations.literal: He found it strange.
translations.idiomatic: He thought it most peculiar.

sentences.zh: 居無幾何，朔果病死。

translations.literal: Not long afterward, Shuo indeed fell ill and died.
translations.idiomatic: Not long after, Shuo fell ill and died, just as one might have expected.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「鳥之將死，其鳴也哀；

translations.literal: The tradition says: "When a bird is about to die, its song is mournful;
translations.idiomatic: As the saying goes: "When a bird is about to die, its song turns mournful.

sentences.zh: 人之將死，其言也善。」

translations.literal: When a man is about to die, his words are good."
translations.idiomatic: When a man is about to die, his words turn to wisdom."

sentences.zh: 此之謂也。

translations.literal: This is what the saying refers to.
translations.idiomatic: This is precisely what the saying means.

sentences.zh: 武帝時，大將軍衛青者，衛后兄也，封為長平侯。

translations.literal: During the time of Emperor Wu, the Grand General Wei Qing was the brother of Empress Wei and had been enfeoffed as the Marquis of Changping.
translations.idiomatic: In the time of Emperor Wu, the Grand General Wei Qing — brother of Empress Wei — held the title of Marquis of Changping.

sentences.zh: 從軍擊匈奴，至余吾水上而還，斬首捕虜，有功來歸，詔賜金千斤。

translations.literal: He had led the army to attack the Xiongnu, advancing as far as the Yu Wu River before returning, cutting off heads and capturing prisoners. Having won merit and returned, the emperor awarded him a thousand catties of gold.
translations.idiomatic: He had led an expedition against the Xiongnu, pushing as far as the Yuwu River before turning back, taking heads and capturing prisoners. He returned victorious, and the emperor rewarded him with a thousand catties of gold.

sentences.zh: 將軍出宮門，齊人東郭先生以方士待詔公車，當道遮衛將軍車，拜謁曰：「願白事。」

translations.literal: As the general was leaving the palace gate, a man from Qi named Dongguo Xiansheng, who had been waiting as a specialist scholar at the Office of the Public Carriage, blocked the general's carriage in the road, bowed and said: "I wish to present a matter to you."
translations.idiomatic: As the general was leaving the palace, a man from Qi named Dongguo Xiansheng — who had been waiting as a specialist scholar at the Office of the Public Carriage — stepped into the road and blocked the general's carriage. He bowed and said: "I wish to offer a word of counsel."

sentences.zh: 將軍止車前，東郭先生旁車言曰：「王夫人新得幸於上，家貧。

translations.literal: The general halted his carriage, and Dongguo Xiansheng spoke from beside the carriage: "Lady Wang has recently won the emperor's favor, but her family is poor.
translations.idiomatic: The general stopped his carriage, and Dongguo Xiansheng spoke from beside it: "Lady Wang has recently won the emperor's favor, but her family is poor.

sentences.zh: 今將軍得金千斤，誠以其半賜王夫人之親，人主聞之必喜。

translations.literal: Now the general has received a thousand catties of gold. If you would sincerely give half to Lady Wang's family, the emperor will surely be pleased when he hears of it.
translations.idiomatic: Now the general has received a thousand catties of gold. If you were to present half of it to Lady Wang's family as a gift, the emperor will surely be delighted when he hears of it.

sentences.zh: 此所謂奇策便計也。」

translations.literal: This is what is called an ingenious strategy and a convenient plan."
translations.idiomatic: This is what you might call a masterstroke of expedient counsel."

sentences.zh: 衛將軍謝之曰：「先生幸告之以便計，請奉教。」

translations.literal: General Wei thanked him, saying: "The master has been so kind as to tell me this advantageous plan. I will respectfully follow your instructions."
translations.idiomatic: General Wei thanked him: "The master has been kind enough to share this excellent counsel. I shall follow your advice."

sentences.zh: 於是衛將軍乃以五百金為王夫人之親壽。

translations.literal: Thereupon General Wei presented five hundred catties of gold to Lady Wang's family as a gift of congratulation.
translations.idiomatic: And so General Wei presented five hundred catties of gold to the family of Lady Wang as a gift.

sentences.zh: 王夫人以聞武帝。

translations.literal: Lady Wang reported this to Emperor Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Wang brought word of this to Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 帝曰：「大將軍不知為此。」

translations.literal: The emperor said: "The Grand General would not have known to do this on his own."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said: "The Grand General would never have thought of this on his own."

sentences.zh: 問之安所受計策，對曰：「受之待詔者東郭先生。」

translations.literal: When asked from whom he had received this plan, the general replied: "From a scholar-in-waiting named Dongguo Xiansheng."
translations.idiomatic: Asked who had given him the idea, the general answered: "It was a scholar-in-waiting by the name of Dongguo Xiansheng."

sentences.zh: 詔召東郭先生，拜以為郡都尉。

translations.literal: An edict summoned Dongguo Xiansheng, and he was appointed Commandant of a commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor summoned Dongguo Xiansheng and appointed him Commandant of a commandery.

sentences.zh: 東郭先生久待詔公車，貧困饑寒，衣敝，履不完。

translations.literal: Dongguo Xiansheng had long waited at the Office of the Public Carriage, enduring poverty, hunger, and cold; his clothes were worn out and his shoes had no soles.
translations.idiomatic: Dongguo Xiansheng had languished for years at the Office of the Public Carriage, impoverished and half-starved, his clothes in tatters and his shoes falling apart.

sentences.zh: 行雪中，履有上無下，足盡踐地。

translations.literal: When walking in the snow, his shoes had uppers but no soles, and his feet trod directly upon the ground.
translations.idiomatic: When he walked through the snow, his shoes had uppers but no soles — his bare feet trod directly upon the frozen ground.

sentences.zh: 道中人笑之，東郭先生應之曰：「誰能履行雪中，令人視之，其上履也，其履下處乃似人足者乎？」

translations.literal: People on the road laughed at him. Dongguo Xiansheng replied to them: "Who else can walk through the snow and leave prints that look like shoes on top and bare human feet on the bottom?"
translations.idiomatic: Passersby laughed at him. Dongguo Xiansheng retorted: "Who else can walk through the snow and leave tracks that show shoes on top and bare human feet below?"

sentences.zh: 及其拜為二千石，佩青緺出宮門，行謝主人。

translations.literal: When he was appointed to the rank of two thousand shi, wearing a blue-green sash, he went out the palace gate to pay his respects to his former host.
translations.idiomatic: When at last he was appointed to the rank of two-thousand-shi, he strode out the palace gate wearing a blue-green sash of office, and went to pay his respects to his former host.

sentences.zh: 故所以同官待詔者，等比祖道於都門外。

translations.literal: His former colleagues who had been waiting together with him at the Office all came to see him off with a farewell feast outside the capital gate.
translations.idiomatic: His former colleagues from the waiting list all gathered outside the capital gate to see him off with a farewell banquet.

sentences.zh: 榮華道路，立名當世。

translations.literal: He brought glory to the roads he traveled and established his name in his own time.
translations.idiomatic: He rode out in splendor, his reputation made in the eyes of all his contemporaries.

sentences.zh: 此所謂衣褐懷寶者也。

translations.literal: This is what is called one who wears coarse cloth but carries treasure within.
translations.idiomatic: This is what the ancients meant by the man who wears coarse cloth but carries a jewel in his breast.

sentences.zh: 當其貧困時，人莫省視；

translations.literal: When he was poor and in difficulty, no one gave him a second glance;
translations.idiomatic: When he was poor and destitute, not a soul spared him a glance.

sentences.zh: 至其貴也，乃爭附之。

translations.literal: When he became honored, everyone scrambled to attach themselves to him.
translations.idiomatic: But the moment he rose to honor, everyone scrambled to attach themselves to him.

sentences.zh: 諺曰：「相馬失之瘦，相士失之貧。」

translations.literal: The proverb says: "In judging horses, one is misled by thinness; in judging scholars, one is misled by poverty."
translations.idiomatic: As the proverb says: "In judging horses, you are misled by leanness; in judging men, you are misled by poverty."

sentences.zh: 其此之謂邪？

translations.literal: Is this not what the saying refers to?
translations.idiomatic: Is this not exactly what the proverb means?

sentences.zh: 王夫人病甚，人主至自往問之曰：「子當為王，欲安所置之？」

translations.literal: Lady Wang fell gravely ill, and the emperor went in person to visit her, saying: "Your son shall be made a king. Where would you like him to be placed?"
translations.idiomatic: When Lady Wang fell gravely ill, the emperor visited her in person and asked: "Your son is to be made a king. Where would you like his kingdom to be?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「願居洛陽。」
人主曰：「不可。
洛陽有武庫、敖倉，當關口，天下咽喉。
自先帝以來，傳不為置王。
然關東國莫大於齊，可以為齊王。」
王夫人以手擊頭，呼「幸甚」。
王夫人死，號曰「齊王太后薨」。
昔者，齊王使淳于髡獻鵠於楚。
出邑門，道飛其鵠，徒揭空籠，造詐成辭，往見楚王曰：「齊王使臣來獻鵠，過於水上，不忍鵠之渴，出而飲之，去我飛亡。
吾欲刺腹絞頸而死。
恐人之議吾王以鳥獸之故令士自傷殺也。
鵠，毛物，多相類者，吾欲買而代之，是不信而欺吾王也。
欲赴佗國奔亡，痛吾兩主使不通。
故來服過，叩頭受罪大王。」
楚王曰：「善，齊王有信士若此哉！」
厚賜之，財倍鵠在也。
武帝時，徵北海太守詣行在所。
有文學卒史王先生者，自請與太守俱，「吾有益於君」，君許之。
諸府掾功曹白云：「王先生嗜酒，多言少實，恐不可與俱。」
太守曰：「先生意欲行，不可逆。」
遂與俱。
行至宮下，待詔宮府門。
王先生徒懷錢沽酒，與衛卒仆射飲，日醉，不視其太守。
太守入跪拜。
王先生謂戶郎曰：「幸為我呼吾君至門內遙語。」
戶郎為呼太守。
太守來，望見王先生。
王先生曰：「天子即問君何以治北海令無盜賊，君對曰何哉？」
對曰：「選擇賢材，各任之以其能，賞異等，罰不肖。」
王先生曰：「對如是，是自譽自伐功，不可也。
願君對言，非臣之力，盡陛下神靈威武所變化也。」
太守曰：「諾。」
召入，至于殿下，有詔問之曰：「何於治北海，令盜賊不起？」
叩頭對言：「非臣之力，盡陛下神靈威武之所變化也。」
武帝大笑，曰：「於呼！
安得長者之語而稱之！
安所受之？」
對曰：「受之文學卒史。」
帝曰：「今安在？」
對曰：「在宮府門外。」
有詔召拜王先生為水衡丞，以北海太守為水衡都尉。
傳曰：「美言可以市，尊行可以加人。
君子相送以言，小人相送以財。」
魏文侯時，西門豹為鄴令。
豹往到鄴，會長老，問之民所疾苦。
長老曰：「苦為河伯娶婦，以故貧。」
豹問其故，對曰：「鄴三老、廷掾常歲賦斂百姓，收取其錢得數百萬，用其二三十萬為河伯娶婦，與祝巫共分其餘錢持歸。
當其時，巫行視小家女好者，云是當為河伯婦，即娉取。
洗沐之，為治新繒綺縠衣，閒居齋戒；
為治齋宮河上，張緹絳帷，女居其中。
為具牛酒飯食，行十餘日。
共粉飾之，如嫁女床席，令女居其上，浮之河中。
始浮，行數十里乃沒。
其人家有好女者，恐大巫祝為河伯取之，以故多持女遠逃亡。
以故城中益空無人，又困貧，所從來久遠矣。
民人俗語曰『即不為河伯娶婦，水來漂沒，溺其人民』云。」
西門豹曰：「至為河伯娶婦時，願三老、巫祝、父老送女河上，幸來告語之，吾亦往送女。」
皆曰：「諾。」
至其時，西門豹往會之河上。
三老、官屬、豪長者、裏父老皆會，以人民往觀之者三二千人。
其巫，老女子也，已年七十。
從弟子女十人所，皆衣繒單衣，立大巫后。
西門豹曰：「呼河伯婦來，視其好醜。」
即將女出帷中，來至前。
豹視之，顧謂三老、巫祝、父老曰：「是女子不好，煩大巫嫗為入報河伯，得更求好女，后日送之。」
即使吏卒共抱大巫嫗投之河中。
有頃，曰：「巫嫗何久也？
弟子趣之！」
復以弟子一人投河中。
有頃，曰：「弟子何久也？
復使一人趣之！」
復投一弟子河中。
凡投三弟子。
西門豹曰：「巫嫗弟子是女子也，不能白事，煩三老為入白之。」
復投三老河中。
西門豹簪筆磬折，向河立待良久。
長老、吏傍觀者皆驚恐。
西門豹顧曰：「巫嫗、三老不來還，柰之何？」
欲復使廷掾與豪長者一人入趣之。
皆叩頭，叩頭且破，額血流地，色如死灰。
西門豹曰：「諾，且留待之須臾。」
須臾，豹曰：「廷掾起矣。
狀河伯留客之久，若皆罷去歸矣。」
鄴吏民大驚恐，從是以後，不敢復言為河伯娶婦。
西門豹即發民鑿十二渠，引河水灌民田，田皆溉。
當其時，民治渠少煩苦，不欲也。
豹曰：「民可以樂成，不可與慮始。
今父老子弟雖患苦我，然百歲後期令父老子孫思我言。」
至今皆得水利，民人以給足富。
十二渠經絕馳道，到漢之立，而長吏以為十二渠橋絕馳道，相比近，不可。
欲合渠水，且至馳道合三渠為一橋。
鄴民人父老不肯聽長吏，以為西門君所為也，賢君之法式不可更也。
長吏終聽置之。
故西門豹為鄴令，名聞天下，澤流後世，無絕已時，幾可謂非賢大夫哉！
傳曰：「子產治鄭，民不能欺；
子賤治單父，民不忍欺；
西門豹治鄴，民不敢欺。」
三子之才能誰最賢哉？
辨治者當能別之。
